confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed from Iskandar Beg Munshi's Tarikh-i Alam-ara-yi Abbasi — the 1,116-page chronicle written by Abbas's own court secretary, covering the entire reign with year-and-often-month precision. Supplemented by contemporary European visitors (Antonio de Gouvea, Pietro della Valle, the Sherley brothers). Capped at ~55 of the most significant stops; the chronicle records dozens more minor royal movements between Isfahan, Qazvin, Mazandaran, and Ashraf.

sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-i Alam-ara-yi Abbasi (c. 1629)
Antonio de Gouvea, Relaçam em que se tratam as guerras e grandes victorias (1611)
Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids (1980)
Sholeh Quinn, Shah Abbas: The King Who Refashioned Iran (2015)

journey.event: Born at Herat on 27 January 1571, son of Muhammad Khudabanda.
Raised at Herat under his guardian Murshid Quli Khan Ustajlu.
Enthroned as shah at Qazvin on 1 October 1587 at the age of 16 after Murshid Quli Khan deposed his father.
Concluded the humiliating Treaty of Istanbul with the Ottomans, ceding Azerbaijan and Georgia temporarily.
Visited the shrine of Fatima Masumeh at Qum.
Made the first of many pilgrimages to the shrine of Imam Reza at Mashhad.
Crushed the Uzbek khan Din Muhammad at the Battle of Herat in August 1598, reclaiming Khurasan.
Walked the 800 km from Isfahan to Mashhad on foot as a pilgrimage vow before the Uzbek campaign.
Transferred the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan and began the grand rebuilding programme around the Naqsh-i Jahan square.
Safavid forces retook Bahrain from the Portuguese.
Launched the war of reconquest against the Ottomans, beginning with Nakhchivan.
Deported the Armenian population of Julfa to New Julfa in Isfahan (one of the largest forced migrations in the region's history).
Recaptured Tabriz from the Ottomans after 18 years of occupation.
Besieged and took Erivan fortress from the Ottomans after an eight-month siege.
Extended Safavid control westward to Kars.
Crushed a huge Ottoman army at the Battle of Sufiyan in September 1605.
Took Ganja in the eastern Caucasus.
Reconquered Shirvan from the Ottomans.
Recovered Derbent on the Caspian coast.
First of several campaigns against Kakheti and Kartli in Georgia.
Began construction of the royal garden city of Ashraf in Mazandaran as a summer capital.
Received English ambassador Sir Robert Sherley at Isfahan to discuss an anti-Ottoman alliance.
Deposed the Georgian kings Luarsab II and Teimuraz I and installed Muslim converts in their place.
Visited the Safavid ancestral shrine at Ardabil.
Launched a punitive campaign in Kakheti, reportedly killing 60-70,000 Georgians and deporting 100,000 more.
Negotiated peace with the Ottomans at Sultaniyya, ending the second war with favourable terms for the Safavids.
Received Pietro della Valle and other European visitors at the new Naqsh-i Jahan complex.
Captured Kandahar from the Mughals under Jahangir.
Safavid forces supported by the English East India Company took Hormuz from the Portuguese in April 1622, ending 115 years of Portuguese rule.
Recaptured Baghdad from the Ottomans after a brief siege.
Visited the shrine of Ali at Najaf after the capture of Baghdad.
Visited the shrine of Husayn at Karbala.
Visited the twin shrines of the seventh and ninth Imams at Kazimiyya near Baghdad.
Visited the shrines of the tenth and eleventh Imams at Samarra.
Personally supervised the successful defence of Baghdad against an Ottoman counter-offensive.
Held court at Qazvin before the final illness.
Returned to Isfahan; began showing signs of the final illness.
Withdrew to his beloved Mazandaran for the last time.
Died at Ashraf in Mazandaran on 19 January 1629 after a 41-year reign.
Buried in a modest grave at Kashan rather than at the royal shrine at Ardabil, reportedly by his own wish.
